chain_of_thought: Tools are returning 404, so I cannot inspect the native function body or perform the mandated backward data-flow analysis.
The crash info is almost empty: no concrete native frames besides the JNI entry, and the process termination cause is unknown ("??").

The only known element is that the fuzz harness invokes Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor and something crashes. With no stack details, I can’t tie this to a specific memory-safety operation (memcpy, etc.) or even distinguish abort/assert from out-of-bounds.
Realm’s native query descriptors typically parse byte sequences or strings from Java, which in principle could hide vulnerabilities, but without any trace or code I’d be speculating.
Given the triage rules, I should not label a vulnerability without concrete evidence of unsafe behavior and attacker-influenced parameters at the crash site.
Since neither the native code nor the Java call path are available, there is no concrete proof that this is more than a generic crash (possibly harness mis-use, e.g., invalid descriptor format).
I will therefore classify it as not-a-confirmed vulnerability, with low confidence, and note that more detailed stack / code is required for a real assessment.
reasons: No concrete native stack frames or faulting instruction are provided beyond the JNI entrypoint
Cannot inspect librealm-jni.so implementation or locate unsafe memory operations due to tool errors
Crash could easily stem from harness-only misuse (e.g., passing impossible/raw bytes into descriptor parser) rather than a reachable app bug
recommendations: Re-run fuzzing with symbolized native stack trace and exact signal/abort reason
Manually inspect Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor in librealm-jni.so for unchecked parsing or bounds issues
Reproduce crash in an instrumented environment (ASan/UBSan) to see whether it is memory corruption vs. logical abort
assumptions: Fuzzer directly controls descriptor bytes or query string arguments to the JNI method
No additional validation layers exist that would prevent malformed inputs in production paths
limitations: Ghidra MCP APIs returned 404, preventing code-level analysis
No signal type, register dump, or allocator diagnostics are provided
Java-side call graph and argument construction are not available for inspection
